title: CMS Fingerprint + Version — Pin the Exact Build
body:
You found a web app but it's not one with a dedicated node (WordPress/Grav/etc). Two jobs: identify WHICH CMS it is, then pin the EXACT version — because public exploits are version-specific and the version is what turns 'a CMS' into 'CVE-XXXX, run this'.

APP IN A SUBDIRECTORY: the root often looks empty while the real app lives under /test, /cms, /site, /blog. Your dir scan reveals it (a 301 to /test/ with the app inside). Don't conclude 'nothing here' from a bare root — follow the redirect.

FINGERPRINT by signature paths/files: zp-core/ + zp-config.php = Zenphoto; wp-content/ = WordPress; /administrator + /components = Joomla; /user/login + /sites/default = Drupal; a 'generator' meta tag often names it outright.

FIND THE VERSION (in order of reliability): view-source for a generator meta tag; a version.php / CHANGELOG / README / License file (CMSs ship these); the docs folder (Zenphoto leaves doc_files/*.pdf naming the release); the admin login footer. Directory listing enabled on the app's folders hands you all of this for free.

Once you have 'CMS X version Y', go hunt the CVE — that's the searchsploit step.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. App in a subdir? Follow the redirect, map the CMS
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -s http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'generator|powered by|<title'   # meta generator names the CMS
whatweb http://$ip/test/                                        # version guesses + tech stack
# signature paths: zp-core (Zenphoto) | wp-content (WP) | administrator (Joomla) | sites/default (Drupal)

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. Pin the exact version (try these in order)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -s http://$ip/test/ | grep -iE 'ver|version|generator'
curl -s http://$ip/test/zp-core/version.php
curl -s http://$ip/test/doc_files/README.html | grep -iE 'version|release'
curl -s http://$ip/test/CHANGELOG.txt | head
# CMSs leave docs that name the release — Zenphoto: doc_files/*_quick_reference.pdf

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. Directory listing = free loot (your dir scan flagged these)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -s http://$ip/test/zp-data/        # config dir — DB creds in zp-config.php (read on-box later)
curl -s http://$ip/test/uploaded/       # upload dir — file-upload-to-RCE candidate

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4. Fuzz for FILES now that you have the structure (extensions matter)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
feroxbuster -u http://$ip/test -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt \
  -x php,txt,bak,old,sql,zip,conf -s 200,301,302,403 -d 1
warn: A 200 with 0 bytes (like zp-config.php over HTTP) means PHP executed and hid the source — you can't read it remotely, but the on-box file holds DB creds once you have a shell. Hunt config BACKUPS instead (zp-config.php.bak/.old/~) — those serve as plaintext. And keep the status filter on (-s 200,301,302,403) or a large wordlist drowns you in 404s from scraped CMS directory names.

choices.label: Got CMS + version — hunt the CVE
Have an exploit file — run it correctly
Found a login page — try default/brute
Directory listing exposed an upload dir
